title: Do not try to enable AppArmor in rootless mode
body:
When in rootless mode it's not possible to load profiles or check which profiles are loaded. 

I am proposing this change is to avoid users having to specify `--security-opt apparmor=unconfined` every time they are running rootless.

If this is something that makes sense I'll also add some tests.

Actually a better way would be to get the parent process effective capabilities to see whether CAP_MAC_ADMIN is set. CAP_MAC_ADMIN allows controlling AppArmor without root permissions. 
However I am not sure how to do it given that at the check time we are root in the user namespace and calling capget will give full effective capabilities.

Something like:
```diff
-       aaControllable := !rootless.IsRootless()
+       aaControllable := true
+
+       if rootless.IsRootless() {
+               myCaps, err := capability.NewPid2(0)
+               if err != nil {
+                       return fmt.Errorf("Error getting current process capabilities")
+               }
+               err = myCaps.Load()                                                                                                 
+               err != nil {
+                       return fmt.Errorf("Error getting current process capabilities")
+               }
+               aaControllable = myCaps.Get(capability.EFFECTIVE, capability.CAP_MAC_ADMIN)
+       }
```

comments.body: @vrothberg PTAL

The proposed changes make the code really complex. I think it's sufficient to add another check here https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/blob/master/cmd/podman/create.go#L235 and here https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/blob/master/cmd/podman/create.go#L199. 

But I think it's time to put the AppArmor loading into a separate function `func loadAAProfile(profile string) error` (for instance) which would allow us to do a simple `return nil` instead of increasingly complex branches. For the rootless case, I suggest to log that Podman is not loading the AppArmor profile because of the above mentioned reasons.
@giuseppe PTAL
Yes I agree that having a function is probably the cleanest way to do it. I'll do a rewrite.

I agree with @vrothberg, it is better if you could refactor it in a function.

If it is something useful to have, we could add the check for the effective capabilities before doing the re-exec and store that result into an env variable.

If the re-exec code will grow to be too complex we might need to use some other data format instead of env variables, but it is not something for this PR.
@marcov I added the WIP to make sure no one accidently merges it,  Remove it when you are ready to have the PR reviewed.
is it something we could do using directly `prctl` on the current thread instead of going through `/proc`?

> is it something we could do using directly prctl on the current thread instead of going through /proc?

I think that's possible. There's already quite some logic in `psgo/internal/capabilities`. We could sacrifice this package and make it public by moving it to `psgo/pkg/capabilities` and add a new API to do that. @marcov, what do you think?

@vrothberg: I like your idea, but consider there's already a [gocapability](https://github.com/syndtr/gocapability/) package to operate on capabilities (even though this does not give access to the full capability bitmasks)

@giuseppe: `prctl` can only read one capabilty bit per call. I thought that reading the full-mask would be more future proof if we need to get other bits in future.

Can one of the admins verify this patch?
I understand the following commands:
- `bot, add author to whitelist`
- `bot, test pull request`
- `bot, test pull request once`
bot, add author to whitelist
@marcov Then I suggest going with your initial proposal of extending `psgo` with the required API and go from there.
bot, add author to whitelist
yes extending psgo is a good idea, but in our case don't we know exactly what capabilities are needed?

`prctl` is enough for now but we'll need to call it n times if in future we have to check for n capabilities. That's why I proposed reading the full capabilities once only with psgo. Anyway I am fine in both ways.
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably d20f3a5) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.

I actually need to correct myself regarding what is possible to do with AppArmor as non-root user.

The pkg `apparmor` uses `apparmor_parser` as a frontend to AppArmor, and that requires root privileges.

I am sorry for having provided you wrong information.
So for now I'll avoid checking capabilities and just do the refactoring of AppArmor code into a function as per your suggestion.

PS: some more details after checking in the AppArmor kernel code (these are my conclusions and may still be imprecise):
- reading `/sys/kernel/security/apparmor/profiles`  requires to be root user (see `policy_view_capable()` in `policy.c`).
- administering AppArmor requires to be root user **and** CAP_MAC_ADMIN (see `policy_admin_capable()` in `policy.c`).

I've added a few tests in baseline (I could not add them in e2e as they are containered and AppArmor is not usable there, see `IsEnabled()`  in `.../runc/.../apparmor.go`).

@TomSweeneyRedHat  PTAL
changes LGTM, but would like head nods form @vrothberg and @giuseppe
bot, retest this please
Travis failure is network flakes, restarting
Code LGTM. Would like an ack from @TomSweeneyRedHat on the baseline test changes
Note to self, add a little comment in the test for the whale says positioning.
